body.p:
As with other programming languages, it took some time for the optimal application area to crystallize for XSLT. With XSLT too, the internet and web page programming in particular were originally the driving force, since XSLT was particularly good at separating content and semantics.
UWE test footnote (3/40, callout C): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.

Relatively quickly, however, CSS in combination with JavaScript proved to be the standard solution for this purpose. XSLT is now the dominant player in the field of Technical Documentation, and is probably unbeatable here.
body.section:
XML Web Pages Every browser has an XSLT processor built in. It was once very popular to deliver web pages as XMLApplication AreasXML Web Pages and to lay them out using XSLT. Among other reasons, due to the excessive use of JavaScript (also inline), this idea never fully caught on. Eventually XHTML was specified and now there is HTML5. | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . Let us consider the following XML example: Das ultimative Zwei-Kapitel Dokument Kapitel 1 In Kapitel 1 wird kurz gesagt was Sache ist. Um es kurz zu machen, wie der Hase läuft steht in Kapitel 2. Kapitel 2 Hier wird erklärt, wie der Hase läuft. Im Prinzip ist es ganz einfach. Without an XSLT stylesheet assignment, the browser will display a file with this content as indented XML - or simply ignore the tags and render the text content in a single line. If you add a Processing Instruction The Processing Instruction is evaluated by the parser as a command and not as part of the XML content XML ConstructsProcessing Instruction at the beginning, an XSLT stylesheet is invoked by the browser, and the declared XML transformation is executed before rendering in the browser: Das ultimative Zwei-Kapitel Dokument [...] The XML can now be opened in the browser. Everything is displayed nicely formatted... | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , .

The Processing Instruction has no influence on the XML content and is not used in other input processing.

Server-Side Conversion Server-side conversionApplication AreasServer-Side Conversion is also common. An example from the past - WAP pages This technology was used to deliver web content to mobile phones. That was before smartphones for different mobile phone models. In the past, mobile phones had very different display sizes. Mobile browsers could not adequately handle JavaScript, and the scaling of the WAP page for the respective phone did not happen on the phone itself, but on the server side before delivery. For this purpose, an XML source was transformed into different WML WAP representations using various XSLT stylesheets. This is how the two-chapter example from above would look in WML format (kept quite simple):

In Kapitel 1 wird kurz gesagt was Sache ist.

Um es kurz zu machen, wie der Hase läuft steht in Kapitel 2.

Hier wird erklärt, wie der Hase läuft.

Im Prinzip ist es ganz einfach.

 An XSLT transformation that converts the XML data from above into this WML representation could, for example, be implemented as follows: | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , .

Multiple Output Formats | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , .
From a single XML source, additional formats can also easily be generated, e.g. EPUB EPUB. This is the standard format for eBooks. In addition to tags for content formatting, there are also instructions for generating the table of contents or other navigation structures.
Other common formats besides the now obsolete WML format include electronic output formats such as: CHM Windows Help, EclipseHelp Eclipse Help, JavaHelp JavaHelp, ..., print output formats such as PDF or Adobe Framemaker Framemaker, or XML standard exchange formats such as DITA, S1000D, PI-MOD PI-MOD, JATS JATS StandardsJATS or TEI TEI StandardsTEI .

section.title: Human-Readable Output
section.p: Cryptic XML log, data or configuration filesApplication AreasLog and Configuration Data can easily be formatted to be "human-readable" with XSLT. A colleague at my new job recently approached me asking if I knew of a way to format his cryptic data file for a translation service:
section.pre: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>?<?xml-stylesheet type="text/xsl" href="de.xsl"?> <jcr:root xmlns:sling="http://sling.apache.org/jcr/sling/1.0" xmlns:jcr="http://www.jcp.org/jcr/1.0" xmlns:mix="http://www.jcp.org/jcr/mix/1.0" xmlns:nt="http://www.jcp.org/jcr/nt/1.0" jcr:language="de" jcr:mixinTypes="[mix:language]" jcr:primaryType="sling:Folder"> <b_manual jcr:primaryType="sling:MessageEntry" sling:message="Bedienungsanleitung"/> <b_warning jcr:primaryType="sling:MessageEntry" sling:message="Warnung"/> <b_danger jcr:primaryType="sling:MessageEntry" sling:message="Vorsicht"/> <b_note jcr:primaryType="sling:MessageEntry" sling:message="Notiz"/> <b_notice jcr:primaryType="sling:MessageEntry" sling:message="Hinweis"/> [...]
section.p: With an attached XSLT stylesheetde.xsl such a data file is formatted as a table:

If you add a bit of JavaScript logic to this example and make the fields for the translations editable using the HTML5 contenteditable attributeAttributescontenteditable , then you would only need a reverse transformation from HTML to XML and you would already have built a small XML editor. This is also how the editor in Tektur works.

body.section:
Displaying Diagrams Since an SVG StandardsSVG graphicApplication AreasVisualization is in XML format, it can also be generated directly using XSLT. Via the HTML5

Then we get three nicely formatted yellow SVG blocks ... Further links: Client-side image generation with SVG and XSLT Automatic image generation with XSLT and SVG Node type visualization in the Apache Jackrabbit project Visualization of nodes in the Apache Jackrabbit project
